title: The Job of a College President
significance: The founding document of learning engineering. Simon coined the term 'learning engineer' and argued that teaching effectiveness is a domain of expertise distinct from subject-matter knowledge, requiring its own scientific foundation in cognitive science. The philosophical and historical origin of everything in the field.

title: Online Education: A Catalyst for Higher Education Reforms
significance: Re-introduced 'learning engineer' to higher education discourse at the moment MOOCs reached 58M+ global students. Recommended universities create dedicated learning engineering roles. Widely cited as the document that triggered the modern LE movement in higher education institutions.

title: High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering
publisher: University of Pennsylvania Center for Learning Analytics
significance: Synthesized a 2020 convening of 100+ academics, policymakers, and practitioners to define ten strategic priorities in three domains: Better LE Infrastructure, Supporting Human Processes, and Better Learning Technologies. The field's most comprehensive contemporary roadmap and primary policy document framing LE's current agenda.

title: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Teaching and Learning: Insights and Recommendations
authors: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Educational Technology
significance: First major federal policy document addressing AI in education with explicit civil-rights framing. Documented algorithmic bias in e-proctoring systems and mandated algorithmic fairness and data representation requirements. Sets the ethical and legal floor for LE practice in the US.

title: EDUCAUSE Horizon Report: Teaching and Learning Edition
significance: The annual predictive literature for edtech adoption across global higher education. Tracks short-, mid-, and long-term adoption horizons. Documents the gradual mainstreaming of LE concepts (analytics, adaptive systems, AI tutors) from forecast to widespread adoption across the 2017–2024 arc.

title: NMC Horizon Report: 2017 Higher Education Edition
significance: Identified adaptive learning technologies and data-driven personalization as short-term adoption trends in higher education. Forecast the long-term restructuring of degrees around competency measurement. Part of the annual Horizon series that documented LE's gradual integration into mainstream institutional practice.

title: Learning Engineering Toolkit: Evidence-Based Practices from the Learning Sciences, Instructional Design, and Beyond
significance: The field's primary practitioner handbook. Frames LE as a verb — an iterative, evidence-based problem-solving process — not a technology set or a job title. Covers lean-agile methods, HCI design, data instrumentation, motivation modeling, and predictive analytics. Technology-agnostic: principles apply equally to AI-driven software and low-tech interventions.

title: Learning Engineering for Online Education: Theoretical Contexts and Design-Based Examples
significance: First book-length treatment of learning engineering applied to digital and distributed contexts. Saxberg's formulation — LE as applying learning science at 'massive, affordable, data-rich scale' — became widely adopted. Documents how online architectures enable continuous telemetry that peer-reviewed journals document years later.

title: Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model (LEAMM)
significance: Organizational capability matrix allowing enterprises to assess and advance their LE practice across human-centered design, learning science integration, and data-driven iteration dimensions. Provides a structured pathway from ad-hoc instructional design toward mature learning engineering practice. The field's primary organizational self-assessment tool.

title: OpenSimon Toolkit
authors: Carnegie Mellon University, The Simon Initiative
significance: Flagship open infrastructure for LE research globally. Comprises DataShop, LearnSphere, CTAT, OLI, DiscourseDB, LightSide, Bazaar, Mathtutor, ChemCollective, and Echo. Enables researchers worldwide to build, test, and study LE systems without proprietary infrastructure.

title: 7 Things You Should Know About Learning Engineering
significance: Widely circulated practitioner-oriented brief in the canonical EDUCAUSE '7 Things' series, introduced LE to a broad institutional technology audience including CIOs, provosts, and instructional technology directors. Accelerated LE vocabulary adoption in non-research higher education contexts.

title: Learning Engineering (IEEE Press)
significance: The canonical textbook of the learning engineering field. Two-part structure covering LE foundations and practice. End notes are a primary seed source for corpus expansion — over 200 references spanning cognitive science, instructional design, analytics, and systems engineering. IEEE Press, 2022. Required reading for any LE practitioner or researcher.
notes: PRIMARY seed document. Harvest all chapter end notes for corpus expansion.

title: IEEE ICICLE Resources Page
significance: Curated resource list maintained by the IEEE ICICLE community. Includes papers, tools, programs, and community resources. The most authoritative practitioner-curated collection of LE resources, updated continuously as the field evolves.

title: How People Learn: Brain, Mind, Experience, and School (Expanded Edition)
authors: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine
significance: The National Academies synthesis of cognitive science, neuroscience, and educational research that established how people learn. Free online. Widely cited as the most authoritative accessible synthesis of learning science. Essential T01 foundation layer and often the starting point for practitioners new to learning engineering.

title: The Forgotten Role of the Learning Engineer
significance: Canonical entry-point essay calling for the learning engineering profession. Argued that education is the 'last great unapplied science' and that learning engineers — practitioners who apply learning science rigorously to educational products — are urgently needed at scale. Widely cited; often assigned as introductory reading for LE courses and programs.

title: ICICLE Body of Knowledge (BoK) — current draft
significance: The field's emerging canonical definition document. Defines competency areas, vocabulary, and practice standards for learning engineering. Provides the official LEBOK framework that this corpus is organized to support. Version tracking required — update when new versions released.

title: MIL-STD-1472H: Human Engineering Design Criteria for Military Systems
significance: DoD standard specifying human engineering design criteria for military systems, equipment, and facilities. The authoritative source for human factors requirements in defense training systems. Directly applicable to LE practitioners designing or procuring training systems in military and government contexts.

title: NASA Human Systems Integration Practitioner's Guide
significance: NASA's operational guide to implementing HSI across the systems engineering lifecycle. Demonstrates how human-centered analysis (task analysis, workload, error analysis) integrates with traditional SE milestones. Applicable to complex LE system development in high-consequence environments.

title: INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook: A Guide for System Life Cycle Processes and Activities (4th ed.)
significance: The authoritative practitioner reference for systems engineering processes and lifecycle management. Provides the SE process framework (requirements, architecture, integration, verification, validation) that learning engineering adapts for learning system development. Essential for LE practitioners with systems engineering backgrounds.

title: DoD Instruction 1322.26: Development, Management, and Delivery of Distributed Learning
significance: DoD policy governing the design, management, and delivery of distributed (technology-based) learning for military training. Mandates ADDIE-based instructional systems design, learning objective taxonomies, and evaluation standards. The governance framework for LE deployment across the largest single training system in the world.

title: State of the Industry Report
significance: Annual survey of corporate training investment, delivery modalities, and effectiveness measurement across hundreds of organizations. Provides LE practitioners with benchmarks for training hours, costs, technology adoption, and evaluation maturity. The most widely cited industry data on the state of organizational learning and development.

title: AC 120-51E: Crew Resource Management Training
significance: FAA advisory circular defining standards for Crew Resource Management (CRM) training programs. CRM training is one of the most rigorous evidence-based training programs ever deployed at scale — a landmark case study in applying learning science to high-stakes professional training. Directly relevant to LE practitioners in aviation, healthcare simulation, and safety-critical training domains.

title: What Works Clearinghouse Handbook (v5.0)
authors: Institute of Education Sciences (IES), U.S. Department of Education
significance: The authoritative evidence standards framework for evaluating education research. Specifies the criteria an intervention study must meet to be considered high-quality evidence (randomized design, statistical power, attrition standards). Sets the evidentiary bar that LE evaluation must meet to influence policy and procurement decisions.

title: Human Systems Integration in the System Development Process: A New Look
significance: National Academies report synthesizing research on integrating human factors into the system development process. Provides the HSI framework (human factors engineering, manpower, personnel, training, safety) that LE borrows when treating learning as a human-system performance problem. Foundational for LE practitioners working in defense and complex system contexts.

title: Siemens Pathway to Learning Engineering
significance: Cloud-based, project-based STEM curriculum and educator professional development introducing engineering design and industry tools for secondary classrooms. Industry-sponsored LE pathway demonstrating how corporate partnerships can fund learning engineering curriculum at scale. Complements university LE pathways.

title: ADDIE Model — Branson et al. (1975) Interservice Procedures for Instructional Systems Development
significance: Adjacent-field resource carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

title: Learning Engineering for Online Education (Dede, Richards, Saxberg eds.)
significance: Edited volume by Dede, Richards, and Saxberg — the primary book-length treatment of learning engineering applied to online education. Covers LE process, adaptive systems, evidence, and scale.

title: Learning Engineering Process Description & Diagram (ICICLE)
significance: Official ICICLE description of the Learning Engineering process, including the iterative cycle diagram showing problem framing, design, measurement, and improvement steps.

title: Learning Engineering Case Guide 1.0 (ICICLE)
significance: Google Slides deck walking through real LE case examples. Shows how the LE process is applied in practice: problem formulation, data collection, evidence-based iteration.

title: Generalizable Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model
significance: PDF defining a maturity model for organizations adopting LE practices. Stages describe progression from ad hoc instructional design through fully evidence-based, data-driven learning improvement.

title: Learning Engineering Implementation Checklist (ICICLE)
significance: Google Doc checklist for practitioners implementing a learning engineering project. Covers project phases from needs analysis through evaluation and dissemination.

title: Examples of Roles & Expertise on an LE Team (ICICLE)
significance: Google Doc describing the multidisciplinary roles typically needed on a learning engineering team: learning scientists, data analysts, instructional designers, engineers, subject matter experts.

title: Learning Design Principles Toolkit (McEldoon, ICICLE)
significance: Google Drive toolkit by Katherine McEldoon connecting cognitive science principles to practical learning design decisions. Bridges learning science theory and LE design practice.

title: Bror Saxberg — MIT Festival of Learning Keynote 2023 (YouTube)
significance: Keynote address by Bror Saxberg at the 2023 MIT Festival of Learning. Covers the vision of learning engineering, the gap between learning science and practice, and the role of data-driven design.
notes: Harvested from sagroups.ieee.org/icicle/resources/ Dec 2025. URL is approximate — verify against ICICLE page.

title: QIP Learning Engineering Video Series (YouTube playlist)
significance: YouTube playlist produced by Quality Information Partners featuring practitioners and researchers discussing learning engineering principles, case studies, and field development.

title: Invitation to Learning Engineering Webinar Series (ICICLE, YouTube)
significance: YouTube series of ICICLE-hosted webinars introducing the learning engineering field to new practitioners, covering foundational concepts, tools, and community resources.

title: IEEE Learning Engineering Podcast
significance: Official IEEE ICICLE podcast series featuring conversations with learning engineering researchers, practitioners, and field leaders. Available on PodServe and Spotify.

title: Silver Lining for Learning — Art & Science of Learning Engineering (podcast, 2023)
significance: Episode of the Silver Lining for Learning podcast exploring learning engineering as both an art and a science — balancing rigorous measurement with creative instructional design.

title: Silver Lining for Learning — Human-Centered Methods in LE (podcast, 2023)
significance: Episode of the Silver Lining for Learning podcast focused on human-centered design methods applied within the learning engineering process.

title: Baker, Boser & Snow (2022) — Learning Engineering: A View on Where the Field Is At
significance: Open-access article surveying the state of the learning engineering field as of 2022. Reviews definitions, practice norms, evidence standards, and key open problems.
notes: Harvested from sagroups.ieee.org/icicle/resources/ Dec 2025. APA Open access.

title: Craig et al. (2023) — Learning Engineering Perspectives for Educational Systems
significance: Multi-author article framing how learning engineering perspectives can transform educational systems at scale. Addresses policy, infrastructure, and community dimensions.

title: Lee (2023) — Learning Sciences & Learning Engineering: Natural or Artificial Distinction?
significance: Scholarly article examining the relationship between learning sciences and learning engineering. Argues for integration rather than separation, tracing epistemological differences and convergences.

title: Kolodner (2023) — Learning Engineering: What It Is, Why I'm Involved
significance: Personal essay and scholarly reflection by Janet Kolodner on the nature of learning engineering, its roots in learning sciences, and her reasons for engagement with the field.

title: 7 Things You Should Know About Learning Engineering (EDUCAUSE, 2018)
significance: Accessible EDUCAUSE brief introducing learning engineering to higher-education technologists and administrators. Covers definition, typical activities, prospects, and challenges.

title: High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering (Baker & Boser, 2021)
significance: Report by Ryan Baker and Beth Boser identifying the highest-leverage opportunities for learning engineering investment. Covers evidence gaps, scale barriers, and field infrastructure needs.

title: The Learning Agency — EdSurge LE Series
significance: Collection of practitioner-oriented articles from The Learning Agency published in EdSurge. Covers LE field development, practitioner perspectives, and connections to education reform.

title: AECT Learning Engagement Activated Podcast — Jim Goodell Episode (2022)
significance: Podcast episode featuring Jim Goodell discussing learning engineering principles, the ICICLE community, and the future of evidence-based learning design in professional development contexts.

title: Goodell (2019) — Are You Doing Learning Engineering or Instructional Design?
significance: Practitioner article by Jim Goodell drawing the distinction between instructional design and learning engineering. Emphasizes data use, iteration, and empirical grounding as key differentiators.

title: Learning Engineering at a Glance (Schatz, Goodell & Kessler, Army Press, 2023)
significance: Concise overview of learning engineering for military and defense training contexts, published by US Army Press. Bridges LE field concepts with high-consequence domain workforce applications.

title: State of XR & Immersive Learning Report 2021 (iLRN / ICICLE)
publisher: Immersive Learning Research Network / IEEE ICICLE
significance: Annual state-of-the-field report on extended reality (XR/VR/AR) in learning contexts, co-produced with ICICLE. Covers research landscape, practitioner adoption, and evidence base for immersive learning.

title: Data Use Throughout the Learning Engineering Process (ICICLE graphic)
significance: Infographic and supporting documentation mapping how different types of data — formative, summative, behavioral, survey — are collected and used at each stage of the LE process.

title: Learning Engineering Adjacent Academic Programs Directory (ICICLE)
significance: Google Doc directory of academic programs adjacent to or incorporating learning engineering, including LE-specific degrees, learning sciences programs, educational technology programs, and HCI programs.

title: Learning Engineering is a Team Sport (ICICLE presentation, 2024)
authors: Learning Engineering is a Team Sport
significance: ICICLE presentation emphasizing the collaborative, multidisciplinary nature of learning engineering teams. Addresses how different domain experts contribute to LE projects and how to build effective teams.

title: Teaming Up to Improve Medical & Healthcare Education (Kurzweil & Marcellas, 2020)
significance: Case study and practitioner article applying learning engineering team approaches to medical and healthcare professional education. Demonstrates LE in a high-stakes, complex domain.

title: Build a Learning Data Dream Team (Torrance, Lin & Goodell, ATD, 2024)
authors: Build a Learning Data Dream Team
significance: Practitioner article on assembling multidisciplinary teams to leverage learning data effectively. Covers roles (data analyst, learning engineer, instructional designer) and collaborative workflows.

title: IDEO Design Thinking Process (ideou.com)
significance: IDEO's publicly available overview of the human-centered design thinking process. Referenced in LE as a precursor and complement to the iterative LE process, particularly for empathy and problem framing stages.

title: Van Campenhout et al. (2023) — Student-Centered Educational Data Science Through Learning Engineering
significance: Research article integrating educational data science methods within a learning engineering framework. Demonstrates how student-centered analysis and iterative design reinforce each other in practice.

title: Herb Simon (1967) — The Job of a College President
publisher: Herbert A. Simon / Google Drive PDF
significance: Archival essay by Herbert A. Simon — foundational thinker behind cognitive science and bounded rationality — on university leadership and the role of evidence in educational decision-making. Cited as a historical intellectual ancestor of learning engineering.
notes: Harvested from sagroups.ieee.org/icicle/resources/ Dec 2025. Historical provenance document.

title: Advancing Workforce Learning through Learning Engineering
publisher: IEEE ICICLE — Invitation to LE Webinar Series
significance: On October 23rd, 2025, the ICICLE Workforce Market Interest Group (MIG) led a webinar on Advancing Workforce Learning through Learning Engineering. The MIG co-chairs, DJ Jaeger and John Costa, explored how learning engineering supports workforce transformation through data-informed design, system-level thinking, and evidence-based practices that link learning to performance outcomes.

title: AECT Learner Engagement Activated — Jim Goodell episode
significance: Fensie interviews Goodell in a long-form format — origin story, ICICLE's founding, and the case for LE as an engineering discipline. If you want biographical context on one of the field's anchors, start here.

title: All Rise the Learning Engineers
significance: An early (2018) public conversation about the LE role. Useful as a time-capsule: what the field thought it was before ICICLE consolidated. Barr and Robson are both early movers; the episode holds up.

title: Applying Learning Engineering in a Global Context
publisher: IEEE ICICLE — Invitation to LE Webinar Series
significance: An Invitation of Learning Engineering held on February 25, 2026, explored how learning engineering is adapted globally in a local context. Hosted by the Globalisation Special Interest Group, Carolina Ali Fojaco, Chief Learning Officer at Project Alianza, provided a deep dive on how they used learning engineering to iterate and scale AnGo, an offline, AI-assisted literacy app to help children in Central America build foundational reading skills.

title: Applying Learning Engineering in Mission-Critical Environments
publisher: IEEE ICICLE — Invitation to LE Webinar Series
significance: The IEEE ICICLE Government and Military Market Interest Group (MIG) led by MIG co-chairs, Eric Ultes and Jason Virtue, held on July 18th, 2025, explored how learning engineering can address learning transfer challenges in mission-critical settings and support evidence-informed training, innovation, and performance through a case study featuring Kevin Owens from the Applied Research Laboratories at the University of Texas, a U.S. Department of Defense University Affiliated Research Center (UARC).

title: Crazy Idea: What If We Use Engineering to Develop and Deliver Engineering Education?
significance: Goodell in conversation with Azad (IFEES) on the provocative inverse of our normal framing: if LE is how we rigorously teach people to engineer, why don't we use LE to teach engineering itself? A 2025 entry worth watching for the international-engineering-education perspective.

title: Discover the World of Learning Engineering in Post-Secondary Contexts
publisher: IEEE ICICLE — Invitation to LE Webinar Series
significance: The Invitation to Learning Engineering held on March 26th, 2025, explored the application of learning engineering across post-secondary contexts. This session highlighted use cases from ICICLE Higher Ed Market Interest Group members, Marilu Lam-Herrera from CPA Western School of Business and Lauren Totino and Aaron Kessler from MIT.

title: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2019
significance: The inaugural ICICLE conference -- the opening public record of the learning engineering community's annual meeting and the baseline for how the field has evolved since.

title: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2022
significance: 2022 edition of the ICICLE annual conference. Program, proceedings, and recorded sessions available.

title: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2023
significance: 2023 edition of the ICICLE annual conference. Program, proceedings, and recorded sessions available.

title: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2024
significance: The field's flagship annual gathering. The 2024 ICICLE conference is the most recent edition, with proceedings, recorded sessions, and the latest practitioner and research presentations.

title: Learning Engineering: A path to empowering learners in and for the Age of AI — Panel
publisher: Empowering Learners for the Age of AI Conference
significance: Panel response to the McNamara and Garg keynotes above. Worth watching as a trio — the panel is where the rough edges of AI-in-LE show up (evaluation, equity, disciplinary identity).

title: Learning Engineering in the Age of AI — Kumar Garg
publisher: Empowering Learners for the Age of AI Conference
significance: Garg (Walton-funded LE efforts; formerly OSTP) frames AI-and-LE as an evidence and infrastructure problem, not a pedagogical one. The pitch: the field needs many more studies, faster, to match AI's pace — and LE is the discipline set up to do that.

title: Learning Engineering: What We Know, What We Can Do — Bror Saxberg keynote
significance: Saxberg's clearest public statement of the learning-engineer role: why instructional design's compliance model falls short at scale, what evidence-based iteration actually looks like in industry, and where the next generation of LEs needs to come from. The canonical 'what is LE' talk for newcomers.

title: Leveraging AI and Learning Engineering in Large-Scale Learning Sciences — Danielle McNamara
publisher: Empowering Learners for the Age of AI Conference
significance: McNamara's case for AI-enabled learning engineering at scale — grounded in her two decades of NLP-and-learning-sciences work. Argues that the most promising AI applications in LE aren't content generation but formative measurement and adaptive scaffolding. Technical but accessible.

title: Silver Lining for Learning — Applying Human-Centered Engineering Methods to Learning
significance: Kolodner, Goodell, and Schatz on where human-centered design methods actually fit inside the LE process — which ones matter, which get applied lazily, and where the practitioner discipline still has gaps.

title: Silver Lining for Learning — The Art and Science of Learning Engineering
significance: Roscoe, Craig, and McNamara on what makes LE distinct from adjacent fields — and where art/intuition still lives inside what's meant to be an engineering discipline. A more philosophical companion to the ICICLE 2024 materials.

title: Unlock the Learning Engineer in You — pK-12 Context
publisher: IEEE ICICLE — Invitation to LE Webinar Series
significance: The Invitation to Learning Engineering webinar held on December 11th, 2024, highlighted a use case from Carnegie Learning on the application of learning engineering in pK-12 context. The case study featured Steve Ritter (Founder & Chief Scientist), Husni Almoubayyed (AI Engineering Manager), and Rae Bastoni (Senior UX Researcher). They showcased insights from the work on facilitating math learning with adaptive reading supports.

title: Examples of Roles and Areas of Expertise on a LE Team
2020 EDUCAUSE Horizon Report — Teaching and Learning Edition
Bridging learning research and teaching practice for the public good: The learning engineer
Data-Informed Course Improvement: The Application of Learning Engineering in the Classroom
significance: Higher-ed classroom case study. Fensie walks through a specific course intervention, what data moved, and what didn't — an honest practitioner paper at a major learning sciences venue.

title: Designing a Student Progress Panel for Formative Practice: A Learning Engineering Process
significance: A compact case study of the LE process applied to a real adaptive-learning product (Acrobatiq). Worth reading back-to-back with Fensie's ICLS piece on Data-Informed Course Improvement.

title: Designing for Transfer: Developing a Skill-Based Simulation Using Learning Engineering Design Frameworks
significance: MODSIM paper on building a skills simulator with transfer as the explicit design target. Good example of LE-in-defense-adjacent training contexts where simulators are the primary medium.

title: LE Toolkit — Introduction (open access chapter)
LE Toolkit — Learning Engineering Applies the Learning Sciences (open access chapter)
LE Toolkit — Learning Engineering is a Process (open access chapter)
Learning Engineering Enlightenment: Think Like an Engineer
significance: Wagner's 2024 follow-on to her long thread of LE-vs-ID articles. The sharpest version of her argument that the shift isn't tools, it's disposition: LEs reason like engineers about uncertainty and evidence.

title: The Science of Remote Learning
significance: Pandemic-era compilation from MIT Open Learning distilling what the learning sciences say about effective remote instruction. Practical, evidence-based, and widely shared during the 2020 pivot.

title: Teaching Online Preparation Toolkit (TOPkit)
significance: UCF's open toolkit for preparing faculty to teach online — widely referenced as a pragmatic, institutionally-scaled resource. ICICLE points to it from their resources page; it's one of the few real-world implementations of LE-style staff development at scale.
